coredata.dc:title: Cu(II)-assisted orange/green dual-emissive carbon dots for the detection and imaging of anthrax biomarker
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Pang, Lan-Fang
Wu, Hao
Wei, Meng-Xia
Guo, Xiao-Feng
Wang, Hong
coredata.dc:description: The spores of Bacillus anthracis are highly deadly to human beings and animals, and are concurrently potential biological warfare agents. Hence, the rapid and sensitive monitoring Bacillus anthracis biomarker, dipicolinic acid (DPA), is very desirable. Herein, orange/green dual-emissive carbon dots (OG-CDs) were synthesized via the hydrothermal approach. The OG-CDs not only emitted dual fluorescence at 527 and 590 nm under the single 503 nm excitation, but also exhibited excellent water solubility, good photostability and great salt tolerance. The fluorescence of the OG-CDs at 527 nm can be completely quenched when chelated with Cu(II). However, because of the stronger chelation between DPA and Cu(II), the fluorescence restored rapidly on subsequent addition of DPA. As such, the CD-Cu(II) system can be used for determination of DPA based on the fluorescence “off-on” response. Under optimum conditions, the detection limit for DPA was 56 nM, with a linear range of 0.5–12.5 μM. The established CD-Cu(II) based spectrofluorometric method has been applied to the analysis of DPA in real water samples with recoveries of 93.6%–104.3%. More remarkably, the CD-Cu(II) probe also has been successfully applied for the imaging of DPA in Escherichia coli with excellent bio-compatibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-emissive carbon dot
Nanomaterial
Fluorescent probe
Fluorescence “off-on” response
Bio-imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of DPA using CD-Cu(II) system
Detection of DPA in real samples
Design thoughts and choice of materials
Assay of DPA in real samples
Application of the OG-CDs in biological imaging

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images and (B) particle size distribution of the OG-CDs.
FT-IR spectrum of the OG-CD sample.
(A) XPS full spectrum of the OG-CD sample. High resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (B), N 1s (C), and O 1s (D).

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the OG-CDs. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs (50 μg·mL−1) under different excitation wavelengths from 420 to 510 nm. (C) Fluorescence decay curves of the OG-CDs (50 μg·mL−1) in the absence and presence of Cu(II) (2.5 μM).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CD-Cu(II) system upon addition of different concentrations of DPA (0–30 μM). The concentration of the CDs is 50 μg·mL−1, and the concentration of Cu(II) is 6 μM. (B) The linear relationship between I527/I590 and the DPA concentration from 0.5 to 12.5 μM. All the tests were carried out in HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4), excitation wavelengths is 503 nm.

(A) Fluorescence intensities of the CD-Cu(II) system alone and after the addition of 125 μM various relevant analytes. The concentration of DPA is 12.5 μM. (B) The influence of other relevant analytes (12.5 μM, except the concentration of Fe3+ is 5 μM) on the detection of CD-Cu(II) system toward DPA (12.5 μM). The concentration of the CDs is 50 μg·mL−1, and the concentration of Cu(II) is 6 μM. All the tests were carried out in HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4), excitation wavelengths is 503 nm.

Confocal fluorescence images of DPA in E. coli bacteria. (A, E, I) Bright field images. (B, F, J) Green channel images (λex = 488 nm) of E. coli bacteria incubated with OG-CDs (50 μg·mL−1), OG-CDs (50 μg·mL−1) + Cu(II) (3 μM), and OG-CDs (50 μg·mL−1) + Cu(II) (3 μM) + DPA (30 μM), respectively. (C, G, K) Orange channel images (λex = 488 nm) of E. coli bacteria. (D, H, L) Merge green channel and orange channel images.
Schematic diagram of the preparation of the CD-Cu(II) probe and its application for the detection and imaging of DPA in E. coli bacteria.
Determination of DPA in lake water and tap water samples.

head.ce:title: Cu(II)-assisted orange/green dual-emissive carbon dots for the detection and imaging of anthrax biomarker

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education)
College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430072, P. R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this manuscript.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The spores of Bacillus anthracis are highly deadly to human beings and animals, and are concurrently potential biological warfare agents. Hence, the rapid and sensitive monitoring Bacillus anthracis biomarker, dipicolinic acid (DPA), is very desirable. Herein, orange/green dual-emissive carbon dots (OG-CDs) were synthesized via the hydrothermal approach. The OG-CDs not only emitted dual fluorescence at 527 and 590 nm under the single 503 nm excitation, but also exhibited excellent water solubility, good photostability and great salt tolerance. The fluorescence of the OG-CDs at 527 nm can be completely quenched when chelated with Cu(II). However, because of the stronger chelation between DPA and Cu(II), the fluorescence restored rapidly on subsequent addition of DPA. As such, the CD-Cu(II) system can be used for determination of DPA based on the fluorescence “off-on” response. Under optimum conditions, the detection limit for DPA was 56 nM, with a linear range of 0.5–12.5 μM. The established CD-Cu(II) based spectrofluorometric method has been applied to the analysis of DPA in real water samples with recoveries of 93.6%–104.3%. More remarkably, the CD-Cu(II) probe also has been successfully applied for the imaging of DPA in Escherichia coli with excellent bio-compatibility.

Schematic diagram of the preparation of the CD-Cu(II) probe and its application for the detection and imaging of DPA in E. coli bacteria.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Anthrax is an acute contagious disease caused by Bacillus anthracis (B. anthracis) [1,2]. The spores of B. anthracis are commonly exit in soil and can survive at environmental extremes, such as strong acid, strong base, heat, radiation, freeze, and desiccation [3–5]. When contacted with B. anthracis, its spores is activated and then release toxic substances which can give rise to swelling, internal bleeding, and even tissue death [6,7]. Inhalation of over 104 B. anthracis spores is deadly unless received treatment in 24–48 h [8,9]. In addition, B. anthracis spores can be used as potential biological warfare agents by terrorists, which have caused great concern all over the world [10–12]. As 2,6-dipicolinic acid (DPA) is a major chemical constituent of B. anthracis spores and not found in other common spores or environmental materials, it is regarded as a typical anthrax biomarker [13,14]. Thus, developing rapid, sensitive and accurate methods to detect DPA in diverse samples matrix is vital for anthrax disease or bioterrorism prevention.

As an emerging star in the nanocarbon family, carbon dots (CDs) with superiority over traditional semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes in terms of superior fluorescence properties, good water solubility, excellent bio-compatibility and low toxicity [15,16]. These excellent properties of CDs have enabled them favorably employed in many promising applications, particularly sensing and bio-imaging [17,18]. However, although many kinds of CDs have been reported till now, most of them displayed a single blue or green fluorescence under excited with ultraviolet (UV) light. Owing to severe biological tissue damage under UV light exposure and universal biorelated blue auto-fluorescence interference, blue or green emissive CDs are not perfect candidates for bio-imaging studies [19,20]. On the contrary, the CDs with a long emission wavelength and unique dual fluorescence emission property have the advantages in bio-imaging field and can offer a glimmer of hope for preparing modification-free ratiometric probe [21,22]. As such, in view of the performance and practical applications of the CD-based fluorescent probes, it is significant to develop dual-emissive CDs with long emission wavelengths for biological analytes.

Up till now, there are still no CD-based fluorescent probe applied to the bio-imaging of DPA, but some studies based on CDs for the spectrofluorometric determination of DPA in real samples have been performed. Thereinto, the vast majority of these DPA probes were prepared through chelating lanthanide ions (Ln(III)) onto the surface of CDs [23–27]. However, the emission intensity of CDs in these probes remained unchanged upon adding DPA, while the emission intensity of Ln(III) acted as the reporter signal to detect DPA though the formation of Ln(III)-DPA complex [26]. Thus, they are more like fluorescent probes based on Ln(III) than CDs. Meanwhile, these kinds of probes possess some limitations. For instance, Ln(III)-target complexes have relatively poor stability under UV light or moisture conditions [28,29]. Moreover, due to the Ln(III) may cause genetic toxicity and DNA damage, the toxicity of such probes exposed to environment and organisms is a serious problem that needs concern [30]. As far as we know, there is only one report on the CD-based DPA probe without Ln(III) decorating till now. Coordinated with Cu(II), an “off-on” CD-based fluorescent probe with emission at 420 nm has been developed for DPA detection in the rain water, tap water and reservoir water samples [31]. The Cu(II)-DPA complex was formed upon addition of DPA, which can disaggregate the CD-Cu(II) complex and the quenched fluorescence of the CDs recovers. However, such CDs emitted blue fluorescence under the excitation of UV light, which might impede their further applications in bio-imaging and biological sample analysis.

Herein, we have prepared orange/green dual emissive CDs (OG-CDs) with facile one-pot hydrothermal method by using p-phenylenediamine dihydrochloride as a single carbon source. The OG-CDs have two fluorescence peaks at 527 and 590 nm respectively under a single excitation wavelength of 503 nm. With the assistance of trace amounts of Cu(II), the CD-Cu(II) system can be served as an “off-on” fluorescent nanoprobe with two sensing channels for the sensitive and rapid detection of DPA (Scheme 1 ). Without introducing Ln(III), this probe entirely relies on the changes in fluorescence intensity of CDs to detect DPA, which fully utilized the advantages of CDs. Significantly, the CD-Cu(II) probe has been successfully used to monitor DPA in Escherichia coli (E. coli).

p-Phenylenediamine dihydrochloride, proline (Pro), methionine (Met) and arginine (Arg) was purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing, China). 2,6-Dipicolinic acid (DPA), o-phthalic acid (o-PA), 3,5-dipicolinic acid (3,5-PA), 2-picolinic acid (2-PA), benzoic acid (BA), p-phthalic acid (p-PA), m-phthalic acid (m-PA) and nicotinic acid (NA) were bought from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai, China). Sodium chloride, potassium chloride, calcium chloride and other inorganic salts were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 3-(4, 5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was obtained from Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiments. The detailed information of apparatus was provided in the Supplementary Material.

Briefly, 27.2 mg p-phenylenediamine dihydrochloride was dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water through ultrasound. This resulting solution was poured into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 200 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, the obtained products were centrifugated (15 min, 5000 rpm) for removal of large particles. Then the red brown supernatant was further purified by filtration with a microporous membrane (0.22 μm). Finally, the purified CDs solution was freeze-dried into powder for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DPA using CD-Cu(II) system
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence tests were carried out in 10 mM HEPES buffer (pH 7.4) at 25 °C. First, 6 μM Cu(II) was added into the 50 μg·mL−1 OG-CDs aqueous solution to form a mixture. Then, DPA with different concentrations (0–30 μM) were separately added into the above CD-Cu(II) system (the final volume is 4 mL). After 2 min, the fluorescence intensity ratio I527/I590 (I527 and I590 are the fluorescence intensities of the CD-Cu(II) system at 527 and 590 nm, respectively) was recorded under the excitation of 503 nm. To evaluated the selectivity of the CD-Cu(II) system toward DPA (12.5 μM), twenty kinds of relevant analytes (125 μM) were added into the system. All the measurements were repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DPA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Recovery tests were conducted in lake water and tap water samples. The lake water was collected from East Lake (Wuhan) and the tap water was obtained from our campus. The water samples (50 mL) were filtered by a microporous membrane (0.22 μm), and then the obtained filtrate was diluted 100-fold with 10 mM HEPES buffer (pH 7.4). For the determination of DPA in water samples, 40 μL OG-CDs stock solution (5.0 mg·mL−1) was added into 4 mL the above pretreated real samples which contained 6 μM Cu(II) and spiked with different concentrations of DPA.

E. coli bacteria and HeLa cells were obtained from College of Life Science, Wuhan University. The MTT assay was conducted to assess the cytotoxicity of the OG-CDs to HeLa cells. Details can be seen in the Supplementary Material.

For imaging of the bacteria incubated with OG-CDs, the E. coli were incubated overnight in LB medium at 37 °C. Then, the bacteria aqueous suspension was centrifuged (10,000 r/min, 3 min) and supernatant was discarded, washed by phosphate buffered saline (PBS, 10 mM, pH = 7.4) twice. 50 μg·mL−1 OG-CDs were used to incubate with the purified bacteria at 37 °C for 12 h. After that, the bacteria aqueous suspension was centrifuged and washed twice with PBS. Finally, the E. coli bacteria were visualized under 488 nm laser excitation by a confocal microscope. For imaging of the bacteria incubated with Cu(II) and DPA, the bacteria loaded with CDs were then incubated with 3 μM Cu(II) for 30 min and then further incubated with 30 μM DPA for 1 h. After that, the bacteria were washed by PBS twice, and then visualized under a confocal microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design thoughts and choice of materials
ce:section.ce:para: At present, some CD-based fluorescent probes for DPA determination in real samples have been developed, but they all have not been used for the bio-imaging of DPA. Thus, we are interesting to design a CD-based DPA probe with superior fluorescence properties that can be applied to the bio-imaging of DPA. According to the report [32,33], the conjugate structures and the amount of amino groups of the precursor maybe played the important role for the preparation of dual-emissive CDs with long emission wavelengths. Besides, we noticed that some precursors of the dual-emissive CDs were hydrochloride or sulfate form [34]. Therefore, to obtain CDs with unique dual fluorescence peaks and long emission wavelengths, we designed the following experiments with several hydrochloride or sulfate as precursors, and these precursors possess different conjugate structures and amount of amino groups.

After the hydrothermal treatment of different precursors at 200 °C for 6 h, the experiment results display in Fig. S1. The CDs synthesized by N-(1-naphthyl) ethylenediamine dihydrochloride, thiamine hydrochloride, and 3,4-diaminobenzoic acid with tyramine hydrochloride show single fluorescence peak at 432, 434 and 580 nm, respectively. While the formed CDs from p-phenylenediamine dihydrochloride exhibit dual emission at 527 and 590 nm respectively under a single excitation wavelength of 503 nm, and these CDs show excellent water solubility, good photostability and great salt tolerance. In addition, the CDs prepared by N,N-diethyl-p-phenylenediamine sulfate salt also emit two fluorescence peaks at 560 nm and 670 nm under 414 nm excitation, but the photostability of the CDs is very poor. As far as we know, the poor photostability of these CDs may be due to the photosensitivity of N,N-diethyl-p-phenylenediamine sulfate salt. On the basis of above research, the p-phenylenediamine dihydrochloride was adopted as the precursor to prepare orange/green dual-emissive OG-CDs for further studies.

Because of the OG-CDs contains abundant surface groups of amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl, it was found that the CDs can bind with Cu(II), accompanied with the complete quenching of the fluorescence at 527 nm while the fluorescence at 590 nm was partially quenched. However, the B. anthracis biomarker DPA can selectively recovered the quenched fluorescence of the CD-Cu(II) complex. Therefore, the CD-Cu(II) system can be used as a fluorescent probe based on the “off-on” fluorescence response for the detection of DPA.

The morphology and size distribution of the OG-CDs were characterized using TEM. As shown in Fig. 1A, the well dispersed OG-CDs with a nearly spherical morphology. Taking 100 particles for the particle size statistics (Fig. 1B), the size distribution range of the OG-CDs is in 4.0–10.5 nm with a mean diameter of 6.1 nm. High resolution TEM image (HRTEM, inset of Fig. 1A) shows a discernible lattice structure of the OG-CDs with 0.23 nm lattice spacing, which agrees with the graphite carbon (100 facet) [35,36]. FT-IR spectroscopy was obtained to investigate the surface groups of the OG-CDs (Fig. 2 ). The absorption peaks at 3424 cm−1, 3132 cm−1 and 1670 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of O−H, N−H and CO, respectively. The peak at 1508 cm−1 corresponds to the CC vibrations in benzene ring. The peaks at 1401 cm−1 and 1016 cm−1 represent C−N and C−O stretching vibrations, respectively. Furthermore, the XPS full spectrum in Fig. 3A shows the presence of C1s (284.7 eV), N1s (401.2 eV) and O1s (532.6 eV), and the corresponding element contents are 64.36%, 12.81% and 14.97%, respectively. The high resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 3B) is decompose into five peaks of C−C/C=C (284.5 eV), C−N (285.0 eV), C−O (285.4 eV), and CO (286.1 eV). Fig. 3C shows the N1s spectrum was fitted to N−H (399.5 eV), C−N (401.0 eV) and C−N−C (401.6 eV). And the CO (531.1 eV), C−O−C (532.3 eV) and C−OH (533.1 eV) are showed in O1s spectrum (Fig. 3D). The characterization results from XPS spectra are consistent with the FT-IR analysis. These above data verified that N has been successfully doped into the OG-CDs, and the excellent water solubility of the OG-CDs attributed to the presence of hydroxyl, amino and carboxylic surface groups.

The UV–Vis absorption of the OG-CDs in Fig. 4A displays two characteristic peaks at around 292 nm and 503 nm. The broad peak at 503 nm is assigned to the n-π* transition of C=O/C−N bonds whereas the shoulder peak at 292 nm is resulted from the π-π* transition of CC [37]. Moreover, Fig. 4B indicates the emission peaks of the OG-CDs are almost invariable under the various excitation wavelengths from 420 to 510 nm, and the maximum emission peaks of the OG-CDs at 527 and 590 nm are observed under the 503 nm excitation. The excitation-independent emission characteristic reveals the OG-CDs have uniform surface state [38]. The fluorescence decay curves are showed in Fig. 4C, and average fluorescence lifetimes of the OG-CDs are around 2.77 and 2.07 ns for 527 and 590 nm emission peaks, respectively. The quantum yield of the OG-CDs was measured to be 5.1% with Rhodamine 6G as the reference.
We also investigated the stability of the OG-CDs under different conditions, including ionic strength, pH and photo irradiation. The detailed content can be seen in the Supplementary Material.

The detection performance of the CD-Cu(II) system toward DPA was investigated under the optimal conditions (details of the conditions optimization was provided in the Supplementary Material, including pH, temperature, reaction time and the concentration of Cu(II)). As displays in Fig. 5A, the emission intensity of the CD-Cu(II) at 527 and 590 nm is increased continuously with the increasing concentrations of DPA (0–30 μM). Fig. 5B shows a good linear relationship between dual emissive fluorescence intensity ratio (I527/I590) and DPA concentration from 0.5 to 12.5 μM (R2 = 0.9895), and a limit of detection (LOD) of 56 nM was obtained based on 3δ/k (δ refers to the standard deviation of blank, and k is the slope of calibration graph). Furthermore, we detailedly compared the performance of the CD-Cu(II) probe with other previously reported CD-based detection methods for DPA. It can be seen from Table S1, the linear range of our probe is wider than most of the reported methods, and the LOD is comparable to some of these methods. More remarkably, this work is the first report on using of CD-based probe for the bio-imaging of DPA.

The responses of the CD-Cu(II) system to various potential interferents including some aromatic acids (e.g. BA, p-PA, m-PA, o-PA, 3,5-PA, NA, 2-PA), amino acids (e.g. Arg, Pro, Met), cations (e.g. K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Ba2+) and anions (e.g. NO3 −, Cl−, SO4 2−) were studied under the optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. 6A, only the presence of DPA (12.5 μM) in the CD-Cu(II) system can cause a significant enhancement of I527/I590, and there is no obvious enhancement of I527/I590 for other analytes (125 μM). The results suggested that the CD-Cu(II) probe has high selectivity toward DPA.

Moreover, we also investigated the effect of these potential interferents on the detection process of the CD-Cu(II) probe to DPA. Fig. 6B demonstrates the changes of I527/I590 induced by DPA (12.5 μM) with twenty different analytes (12.5 μM) together are consistent with which induced by DPA alone (the interference was less than 10%). Therein, the concentration of Fe(III) was set as 5 μM, which is due to the concentration of Fe(III) in river water is about 4 μM, and in drinking water is less than 5.36 μM [39]. These results indicated that the promising application of this probe for DPA determination in real samples.

To investigate the mechanism for OG-CDs quenching by Cu(II), we carried out a series of studies in succession. The fluorescence lifetime decay was firstly investigated (Fig. 4C), and the average lifetime of the OG-CDs at 527 nm is nearly unchanged (from 2.77 to 2.51 ns) before and after the addition of Cu(II). The result suggested that the fluorescence quenching is probably caused by the static quenching process (SQE) [40]. Theoretically, this model can be expressed via Stern-Volmer equation:
F 0
/
F Q
= 1 +
K SV
Q

Therein, F0 and FQ represent the fluorescence intensities of the fluorophore before and after the addition of the quencher, respectively; KSV stands for the constant of the Stern-Volmer, and [Q] represents the concentration of quencher [41].

To confirm the SQE mechanism, we investigated the Stern Volmer parameters at different temperature. As shown in Fig. S4A, the values of KSV (F0/FQ − 1) are decreased gradually with the increase of temperature from 4 to 55 °C (F0 refers to the dual emissive intensity ratio I527′/I590′ of OG-CDs, and FQ refers to the intensity ratio I527/I590 of CD-Cu(II) system). This phenomenon belongs to the SQE and can be attributed to the high temperature prevented the formation of the ground state complex [42]. In addition, the UV–Vis absorption spectra in Fig. S4B show the absorption peak of the OG-CDs at 503 nm disappears upon addition of Cu(II), which further indicates that the SQE occurs and a new non-fluorescence complex between the surface groups of the OG-CDs and Cu(II) is formed [43]. To verify the formation of new complex, we measured the mean particle size of the CDs and CD-Cu(II) system in aqueous solutions by dynamic light scattering (DLS). It can be seen in Fig. S4C, the average size of the CDs increases obviously from 15.7 to 43.8 nm after the addition of Cu(II), which suggests the formation of new complex and thus the particle size increases. In addition, the mean particle size measured by DLS is larger than that measured by TEM. It was mainly because of their different processes of sample preparation. The CDs characterized by TEM are the dried nanoparticles, and the diameter measured is the actual size of the CDs, whereas DLS measures the particle size of the CDs in the solution state, namely hydrodynamic diameter (hydrated state). Thus, owing to solvent effect in the hydrated state, the CDs showed a much larger hydrodynamic diameter [31,44].

According to previous report, DPA can chelate with Cu(II) [45], and the strong coordination between them can remove Cu(II) from the OG-CDs surfaces via the formation of stable Cu(II)-DPA complexes. Thus, the quenched fluorescence can be recovered by DPA. To confirm the Cu(II)-DPA complex was formed, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the CD-Cu(II) mixture after the addition of DPA was studied. As shown in Fig. S4B, upon addition of DPA, the peak of UV–Vis absorption at 503 nm is consistent with the OG-CDs, while an obvious change appears at about 285 nm, implying that Cu(II) has been removed from the OG-CDs surfaces and the Cu(II)-DPA complex is formed. In addition, DLS measured the mean particle size is decreased from 43.8 nm to 18.2 nm after addition of DPA (Fig. S4C). This result further demonstrated that the formation of Cu(II)-DPA complex can remove Cu(II) from the CDs surfaces, and thus the particle size was decreased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of DPA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the practical application of this probe, the assay in real water samples were conducted. As given in Table 1 , no DPA was found in original samples, and standard DPA solutions with different concentrations (3, 5, 8 μM) were added into the lake water and tap water samples to carry out the recovery study. With the obtained calibration plot in Fig. 5B, the concentrations of DPA were determined by the CD-Cu(II) probe. The results in two samples show good recoveries of DPA (93.6%–104.3%) and low relative standard deviations (RSD less than 4.13%), which demonstrate the CD-Cu(II) probe possess high accuracy for practical determination of DPA in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the OG-CDs in biological imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The long wavelength emissive CDs can minimize the background auto-fluorescence interference of biological matrix and photodamage to biological samples. In addition, the CDs with two fluorescence emission peaks could improve the accuracy of bio-imaging analysis like ratiometric fluorescent probes [21,34,46]. Therefore, the orange/green dual-emissive OG-CDs were utilized for bacteria imaging. The cytotoxicity of the OG-CDs was first tested by MTT assay. The results in Fig. S5 reveal the low toxicity of the OG-CDs to HeLa cells due to more than 80% cells are survived after 24 h of incubation with CDs (0–100 μg·mL−1).

Accordingly, to assess the practical performance of the OG-CDs in biological applications, E. coli bacteria were used as the model to study the bio-imaging of DPA using the CDs. As displayed in Fig. 7 , after incubation with the OG-CDs (50 μg·mL−1) for 12 h, the morphology of these bacteria (Fig. 7A) and the bright green and orange fluorescence (Fig. 7B and C) from the bacteria are observed. To further imaging of the DPA in E. coli bacteria, the bacteria loaded with 50 μg·mL−1 OG-CDs were treated with 3 μM Cu(II) for 30 min. Fig. 7F and G shows the green fluorescence decrease obviously, and the orange fluorescence retain partially after addition of Cu(II). The reserved orange fluorescence can still indicate the existence and distribution of the CDs in E. coli bacteria. Then, the above bacteria which loaded with CD-Cu(II) were further incubated with DPA (30 μM) for 1 h. As shown in Fig. 7J, the bright green fluorescence signal restore. These good results indicated that the established CD-Cu(II) fluorescent nanoprobe can be used for monitoring DPA in bacteria with excellent bio-compatibility.

In summary, the dual-emissive OG-CDs with orange and green fluorescence were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal approach. With the assistance of trace amounts of Cu(II), the established CD-Cu(II) nanoprobe can be applied for the sensitive and rapid detection of anthracis biomarker DPA based on the “off-on” fluorescence changes. The response mechanism of this nanoprobe toward DPA may be owing to the formation of Cu(II)-DPA complex with the addition of DPA, which can remove Cu(II) from the CDs surfaces and quenched fluorescence of the OG-CDs restored. Furthermore, the CD-Cu(II) probe was used for determination of DPA in real water samples with satisfactory results. Interestingly, the bio-imaging of DPA in E. coli has also been obtained using the proposed CD-Cu(II) probe.

Lan-Fang Pang: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Hao Wu: Investigation. Meng-Xia Wei: Investigation. Xiao-Feng Guo: Conceptualization, Resources, Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Hong Wang: Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 31670370 and 21777126, Beijing, China).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Supplementary data mainly includes apparatus, cytotoxicity assay, quantum yield (QY) measurements, optical stability research of the OG-CDs, optimization of the detection conditions, choice of materials, the influence of pH/temperature/incubation time, UV-vis absorption spectrum of the OG-CDs and CD-Cu(II) system in the absence or presence of DPA, the average particle size of the OG-CDs and CD-Cu(II) system in the absence or presence of DPA, comparison on reported CD-based methods for the detection of DPA.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118872.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Europium-based fluorescence nanoparticle sensor for rapid and ultrasensitive detection of an anthrax biomarker
Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.
